sentences.text: Yeah, we--
Which, which was sort of nondescript.
The weather was pleasant.
It was spring.
And I think for a brief moment, I enjoyed youth, childhood, a little bit because there were no pressures on me.
Then we moved to Brussels.
Yes, as a matter of fact, both in Spa and in Brussels, but particularly in Spa, our wonderful friends went back and forth between my family in Germany and ourselves.
On Sundays, they would come and visit.
And they would report on what was going on with the family we left behind in detail.

And it was through that very-- they had a very sad mission, because they came to tell us that my Uncle Carl, who was the physician, had tried in vain to get to Israel, which was then Palestine, and saw absolutely no, no way out of-- and he was just not going to be around for deportation.
And so on March 17 in 1940, he took his life in order to escape what would lay ahead.
They also, on these trips over, unbeknownst to me, brought valuable things, jewelry, from my relatives, my grandmother, and my aunt, and uncle, and so forth, in order to help us.
And later, as the story unfolds, my father, at very crucial moments, sold this jewelry in order to obtain passage.
And I'll sort of get to that a little bit later.
But they were faithful.
They came.
And they cheered us up, especially my mother, who tended to be very depressed, and as I said, kept us abreast of what the family was doing.

They also saw to it that even though this could no longer take place in Germany, my uncle was buried in the Jewish cemetery in the town and had a memorial stone on his grave, which was very important for us to know.
And it was done with dignity.
And they brought us a, a photo of it.
And it was comforting that he had a resting place.
I was totally, totally shocked.
And it took me quite a long time to get over the fact that he was no longer there.
And it had a very profound effect on my philosophy and outlook on life, so to speak.
Then we, we moved to Brussels.
And they continued to visit us there and to bring us news as long as it was possible to do that.
At some point, that also ended.
Because it became a lot more risky.
We went to Brussels in-- it was probably May and June of 1939.
Am I correct, or was it 1940?
No, no, it was 1939.
Because for nine months, I went to, to school in Brussels with the nuns.
Because in Brussels, all the schools are religious, Catholic.
And I went to school with the nuns.
My brother went to school with the Jesuit brothers.
And we actually had a very, very fine education.
They were most sympathetic.
And we took part in all the activities surrounding the day, including the religious catechism instructions.
The only thing I wasn't allowed to do was to go to confession first thing in the morning when everybody in my class went to confession.
And I, I, I was very upset about that.
I wanted to go into this little booth and also do what the rest my classmates were doing.
And whenever I asked, what is it that you do when you go into this little booth, they always said, it's a secret.
And not until I was much, much older, I realized what they were telling me that they were telling secrets rather than keeping this as a secret from me.
But at the time, I was really very hurt.
And I felt very left out, because that wasn't explained to me.
Nevertheless, the school experience was good.
My French became very fluent.
And also, we learned Flemish at the same time, which is a, a blend of Dutch and French and German.
And we had to learn both languages.
So it was a bit tough coming in there.
But of course, under no circumstance did I ever want to even let it be known there was German in my background.
It was not popular to be German in any shape, for, or fashion, really.
And in Brussels, we lived in a very small apartment.
At that point, my mother was not well.
And along with going to school, I did all the housekeeping and the shopping.
I was old enough now to take over.
And it was interesting.
We, we cooked a single burner and only had cold water on the tap, which had to be boiled in order to clean the dishes.
It wasn't the easiest way of learning housekeeping.
But so it was.
And that was for, for nine months.
In the spring in April, early April, we were called to, to Antwerp to the American consulate, where finally our papers from the United States had been processed.
We went through rigorous physical health examinations and--
This was done at the American consulate.
Because our sponsor had to not only obligate himself to make sure that we would be absolutely no burden to anyone-- the government, that is, or the taxpayers in the United States.
And so we needed a clean bill of health, which unfortunately, my father really didn't have.
But somehow or other, we squeaked him through.

And as we left the consulate after a full day of questionnaires, and examinations, and paperwork, we had tickets to sail out of Antwerp on May 15th, 1940, and of course, joyfully went back to Brussels and made preparations for this all-important journey that we were going to finally be able to take.
As things happen, on May 10th, the lowlands were invaded.
We woke up to thunder in the sky.
And while standing on the balcony of our apartment house and looking at the sky, unbelieving what, what was to be seen, the back of the house received a bomb and was bombed off.

And I remember standing on the balcony and watching the destruction and ultimately, the carts that were taking away dead people, injured people, and realizing that this was a, a disaster all around for everyone.
I think it was a lot too chaotic really for any explanation.
My father went dashing off to the other part of town to confer with other refugee families that we had gotten to know and trying to think of some route of escape out of this.
Because he felt if, if he were anywhere where the Germans would find him, he would be sent back to Buchenwald.
And so he was very desperate to move ahead.
The last news that we had had, my Uncle Moritz was with his family in Paris, with his wife's family.
And so the logical route escape, we felt, or my father felt, was to, to go to Paris and hook up with family there.
He was convinced after going through World War I that the Germans would not go beyond the Maginot Line.
And with that thought in mind, we set out.

Actually, the first leg of our trip, four other people and ourselves, another family of four, chipped in and paid a pommes frites, which is the, the potato chip people that stand in the street with a cart.
We paid him off to try and drive us in the direction of the border.

We really just barely got to the outskirts of Brussels when the roads were so clogged with people walking, escaping, French troops coming in the direction of Brussels because this-- the British and the French were, at that point, of course, in the war.
And he sort of gave up the ghost.
The car gave up the ghost.
It overheated and died.
And so he was generously paid off.
And he left us to go back to Brussels to his family.
And we continued on with just the barest necessity-- a pillowcase for each with some belongings.
And we walked.
On foot, we walked along with everyone else, heading towards the border.
Along the way, we were strafed We'd have to dive into the ditch.
And on a number of occasions, I found myself next to someone who had been hit and was dead.
And we moved along, night and day.
Some of the good-hearted farmers gave us children milk to drink, gave the adults water to drink.
Because we were a pretty bedraggled bunch, really.
One night, I remember, we were camped in a ditch actually for the night when a farmer came out and said, now, do come in and spend the night with us.
And we spent the night with them in the cellar, where there were two mattresses on the floor.
And the children were allowed to stretch out on the mattresses.
And I remember thinking, you know, how marvelous to be finally laid down on something other than earth and fell asleep, soundly asleep, and did not hear the bombings during the night.
And then we continued on the next day.

And when we finally got to the border-- I really don't remember how many days it took us, probably four, I would think-- we had hoped to, because it was a crowd that was trying to get into France, to kind of go with-- get lost in the crowd and that way be able to get over the French border without too much difficulty.
However, when we got there, they asked people to hold up their identification papers.
And ours, being of German origin, were white.
And the others were blue.
And they picked out all the white and pulled us aside.
And we spent an anxious day waiting on the side while everybody else went through.
And then they put us on the truck and took us off to a internment camp.
And initially, that internment camp was really for German prisoners of war.
The women and children were separated.
There were about 480 women and children, a sizable camp.
And--
I, I'm in the process of trying to find out.
I have not been able to place it.
We-- my father was in a barbed wire area with the German prisoners of war.
And of course, he was terrified and convinced that as soon as they found out that he was Jewish that they would, you know, kill him in his sleep or something less desirable.
And so children were allowed to visit their fathers once or twice a week at the barbed wire.

And we made a big show, my father and I, because both of us were fluent in French, of talking French, appearing French, acting French, and getting the idea across that that was really our nationality.
And here we were in the camp.
Our daily fare was one big bowl of what they call lentil soup.
There weren't many lentils in it, nor anything else.
It was generally a dishwater kind of situation.
But we were grateful.
And some of the bread was moldy.
The thing that I remember perhaps in a most embarrassing and distasteful way was that the hygienic facilities were trenchers with two-by-fours over them.
And whenever you, you needed to go there, which you tried to avoid at all cost, a soldier with a mounted bayonet came with you to make sure that there was no escape.
There was barbed wire.
I will never understand why this system was such, but it was a very difficult thing to deal with, especially for youngsters heading into puberty and all that sort of thing.
As the German army approached then, the French, our French soldiers who guarding us, were giving instructions that they were to walk us inland, away from the approaching German troops.
And in the camp, conditions were really sort of so-so.
There were women that delivered babies.
There were women that had delivered babies while we were on, on our march, or walk, or whatever you want to call it.
It, it was general bedlam.
There were small children, crying children, infants.
At any rate, as this became official, we had to pull ourselves together and started walking towards the coast of France.
And again, it was a very difficult thing.
This time, the men really didn't follow orders.
They mixed with the women and carried children and babies and gave whatever assistance was necessary.
It was hot.
It was dusty.
They were all dirt roads.
And we just walked and walked.
Some people dropped at the roadside and just couldn't make.
The farmers, again, came out and gave us water to drink to help us along.
And we were now coming into the summer.
And it was really quite difficult.
We, at different places, we were again sort of herded into locked facilities that had been deserted so that we would not escape.
And ultimately, we ended up somewhere locked in like that at Dunkirk when the British army embarked to leave for England.
My father had thoughts of getting us children on one of the boats to England, but that didn't work out.
They weren't taking any civilians.
And-- but we heard about the evacuation and the boats that came from England and the heroic thing.
And then we were marched on to Calais, which is also a harbor.
And in Calais, we were locked into what used to be a barn-type dance hall thing, everybody together.
And at one point, I lost my parents.
And I had a great deal of difficulty finding them again.
But we were in this building as Calais was burning, and of course, had the thought that this was very dry wood and would very easily catch on fire and burn as well.
But everything was locked.
So there was no getting out of it.
One night, we went to sleep on the floor in this facility.
And a middle-aged couple, which were sort of pseudo grandparents-- I looked upon them as such-- we laid down together to go to sleep.
And I was next to the woman.
And in the morning when I woke up, they were both dead.
They had committed suicide during the night.
And we woke up because the Germans were-- this is now the conquerors were opening the doors and shouting and coming up with, with pistols waving, and said, everybody out of here.
Everybody out of here.
And as we pulled ourselves together, we heard a great deal of gunfire in the streets.
And we were actually-- my father was actually convinced that as we came out of the door, the stairs, there would be a machine gun.
And it was just, just simply shoot everybody that would come out.
And so we said a very emotional goodbye to each other.
And we were fully aware of what would most likely would happen.
We went down the stairs.
And amazingly enough, that was not it.
There were dead soldiers, dead horses, dead animals and people on the street.
But we were not being shot at.
And so we ducked down an alleyway.
And orders were everywhere.
But nobody really knew which way to go or what to do.
And so my father, together with another father and his family, we, we stuck together.
And we set out in the opposite direction of where the German soldiers wanted us to go and went into every farm that we passed and asked whether they would hide us.
And the answers on all of them was, our barn is already full with refugees.
And there is no room.
And no, you can't.
Ultimately, we came to a farm in [INAUDIBLE] where the farmer said, I, I can't house you on my property.
There's an old, broken down chicory factory just 500 yards from, from the farm.
If you can lodge there, you can work my fields.
And in exchange, I will give you food at night, a boiled potato, a hard boiled egg, and something else, some milk, for nourishment.
And so that's what we did.
We stayed in this, this broken down thing which had, of course, no, no facilities whatsoever.
There was a large shovel at the back door.
We would go into the back yard and dig a hole for hygienic purposes.
And we slept on burlap bags, which were, of course, full of lice, as well at night, you could feel the mice running all over, over us as we were trying to sleep.
And we stayed with the farmer approximately seven, seven weeks.
My job-- the men had to go out and dig in the fields and harvest potatoes and beets and things of that nature.

My job-- and now I'm going on 12-- and the farmer's daughter, who was the same age, our job was to take out little stools and a pail at 5 o'clock in the morning and go out into the field and milk the cows.
I was a fast learner.
I learned how to milk a cow.

What I didn't learn fast enough was how to deal with great, big, brown stallions who used to come up behind the cow and scare the cow away and then drink the milk, [LAUGHS] along with any number of cats and kitties that were very anxious for the milk as well.

And after I got that-- over that initial fear, where a stallion chased my cow, knocked over the pail, and all my milk went into the dewy grass, I learned very quickly to pull out the pail and my stool [LAUGHS] and sort of blended into the fog.
But somehow, I, I don't even think of it as a, as a dreadful ordeal.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we accepted it.
There, there were German soldiers in the area.
We knew that there-- we heard that they were on the road.
We didn't have any really bad reports.
Sometimes people would come from a distance and say, this-- where there were large concentrations of Jewish refugees.
And they would come with unpleasant stories.
But we felt fairly, fairly safe.
And this was our daily activity.
My mother was still not very strong.
She was not well.
And so her job was to be in the kitchen and help the farmer's wife put up vegetables and fruits in cans and jars and things of that nature.
So she was more, more oriented towards household things, rather than the outdoor work.
But everybody else was very busy in the fields.
And one of the things that my friend and I did a lot was we were supposed to gather eggs from the hen house.
And we would stop.

And we would sneak a raw egg and punch a hole in it and have our breakfast, a raw egg, which was like a miracle after not having had any really nourishing food for such a long period of time since we had left Brussels.
We really considered it a treat to be able to have food nearby.
And the farmer's wife, of course, baked her own bread.
And so there was always a slice of bread and jam if we--
Our family spoke French at all times.
I mean, this was a given.
My, my mother was not really terribly good at it.
My brother managed.
But he had an accent.
But my father and I were generally pushed to the forefront, because we were the two linguists.
And we spoke to the farmers and whatever authorities or anybody who might be coming along and questioning what was going on.

Oh, absolutely, oh, absolutely-- I mean, not only hiding our identity, but staying out of sight and to avoid any kind of contact or confrontation, even with the French, with other French farmers in the area.
It was all farm land.
And we, under no circumstance, did we want to create difficulties for our host farmer.

Because he might have been reported to the Germans for harboring refugees and thusly get into difficulties, as, as everyone did, really, who-- along the way everywhere, anybody who helped Jewish refugees was-- they were always endangering their own lives.
And we were certainly very aware of that implication by all means.
So it was a busy existence.
And yet, in a, in a sense, it was a healthy existence.
Because it was a farm and fresh air and such a relief not to be behind barbed wire or locked up in some dirty hall of some sort or another without facilities and so forth.
So it was-- and it was a favorable time of the year it was July, which is very pleasant on the northern coast of France.
And-- We'll stop here and change tape.
Fine.
Thank you.
